slides.title: A New Way of Thinking 🧠
slides.content:
# Object-Oriented Programming

So far, you've been writing **procedural** code — sequences of instructions. **OOP** organizes code around **objects** — bundles of data + behavior.

Think about real life:
* A **Dog** has data (name, breed, age) and behavior (bark, fetch, sit)
* A **Car** has data (color, speed, fuel) and behavior (accelerate, brake)
* A **Student** has data (name, grade) and behavior (study, take_exam)

OOP lets you model these real-world things in code! Each object contains:
* **Attributes** — the data (variables)
* **Methods** — the behavior (functions)

slides.title: Classes vs Objects 🏭
slides.content:
## The Blueprint Analogy

**Class** = Blueprint/Template
**Object** = Actual thing built from the blueprint

```
Class: Dog 📋 (the blueprint)
  ├── name
  ├── breed
  └── bark()

Object: my_dog 🐕 (actual dog)
  ├── name = "Buddy"
  ├── breed = "Golden Retriever"
  └── bark() → "Woof!"
```

You write the class **once**, then create **many objects** from it — each with different data!

You've already used objects: strings, lists, and dicts are all objects with methods!

quizData.question: What is a class?
quizData.explanation: A class is a blueprint/template that defines what data and behavior objects will have.

quizData.question: What are the two main components of an object?
